items.text: Premise A re-confirmed at design depth: the exact accept code path (iteration.py phase table + workstream.transition) is cited, showing where a DoD evaluation would have to hook in and that nothing reads dod.items today.

A verifiable-check schema is designed: the predicate types for v0.1 are fixed (command-exit-zero, file-exists, schema-valid), with their fields, and how they attach to an existing iteration_content dod item (prose + optional check) without breaking the current iteration_content schema or existing iterations.

Accept-time semantics are specified: when accept evaluates checks, what counts as pass/fail, how failure refuses the transition (error shape), and where evaluation runs (cwd, timeout, command execution boundary / safety). [Refusal mechanism (GateError, phase stays handback) and run model (repo cwd, default timeout, trust = same as running tests) are specified in P3.3/P3.4; the EXACT error message/JSON is a named deferred fork.]

Non-verifiable (prose-only) handling is specified: how a DoD item with no machine check is flagged so a caller can route it to human escalation, and whether a mixed DoD (some verifiable, some prose) accepts, refuses, or escalates.

Backward-compatibility + migration stance recorded: existing prose-only iterations must still accept (gated how?), and the v0.1 line is held (no confidence thresholds, no arbitrary predicate languages).
Open forks for the planning iteration are listed (what is decided vs deferred), and scope held: no engine code changed in this discovery.

dod.preamble: P3/L2 keystone, lead discovery. L0.2 already confirmed Premise A (DoD is write-only; accept gates purely on the phase tuple). This iteration deepens that into a concrete design: how a DoD carries machine-verifiable checks, how accept evaluates them and refuses on failure, and how prose-only DoD is flagged for human escalation. Design and decisions only - the build is the execution iteration. Decisions locked in iteration 001 constrain this: autonomous acceptance v0.1 is STRICT (machine-verifiable only; subjective escalates), no confidence thresholds.

handback: P3/L2 keystone design complete, recorded as discovery.json sections P3.1-P3.4 and three logged decisions; no engine code (discovery scope). DESIGN: (1) HOOK - DoD evaluation goes in Workstream.transition() on verb=='accept', before the phase mutation; a failed check raises GateError, which handle_transition already maps to exit 1, so the refusal reuses the existing path with no CLI change. This is also the one function that today never reads the DoD - the exact gap Premise A names, now confirmed as a design fact. (2) SCHEMA - each iteration_content dod item gains an OPTIONAL 'check' object; v0.1 supports exactly three deterministic kinds: command (exit 0), file-exists, schema-valid (via validate_entity). Optional => fully backward-compatible; existing prose-only iterations still validate and still accept, no migration. (3) SEMANTICS - lume gains a machine VETO (refuse accept if any verifiable check fails) but NEVER auto-accepts; accept on all-green remains the operator's decision, exactly as today. (4) AUTONOMY SPLIT - lume also EXPOSES verifiability status (each item verifiable/prose-only; DoD fully-verifiable iff all items have checks); the auto-accept-vs-escalate decision lives in tredl (fully-verifiable + all-green may auto-accept; any prose-only item escalates to a human), matching iteration 001's STRICT-acceptance decision. DEFERRED to planning/execution: the surface (a pure evaluator + a 'lume check' dry-run verb + a status flag), the command-execution model (cwd/timeout/trust; sandbox is a discovery-triggered follow-up, not v0.1), check-result audit/persistence, and the exact GateError shape. RECOMMEND next: open the P3/L2 PLANNING iteration to resolve those forks into an ordered build plan.
self_review: Holds: the design is anchored to real code - the hook point, the GateError->exit-1 path, and the validate_entity reuse were all read in source this iteration, not assumed; backward-compatibility is structural (optional field) not aspirational; and the veto/autonomy split is the load-bearing idea that keeps lume's operator-in-the-loop contract intact while still letting tredl be independent (lume can only REFUSE, never auto-accept). Weak / watch: (1) COMMAND-CHECK TRUST is the real soft spot - running an author-supplied shell command at accept means accept can execute arbitrary code; I recorded the trust assumption (DoD author == operator, same trust as running the test suite) and deferred sandbox/allowlist, but an adversarial reviewer should weigh whether even that is acceptable for an AUTONOMOUS operator, since tredl auto-accepting a DoD whose own command it cannot vet is a hole - this likely needs revisiting before tredl drives accept unattended. (2) I marked the 'accept-time semantics' DoD item done while explicitly deferring the exact error message/JSON shape; the mechanism is specified but the wire format is not, so that item is substantively-but-not-fully complete and is annotated as such rather than silently. (3) This is design only; correctness of the schema-valid/command evaluators is unproven until the execution iteration writes tests. Nothing was struck; all four premises from L0 remain TRUE and the design builds directly on them.
